revision.text:
{{coord|35|25|44.53|N|133|4|25.26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}}
[[Image:Yaegaki jinja.jpg|thumb|right|Yaegaki Shrine.]] {{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]].
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo]]'' and princess ''Kushinada'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the ''Yamato-no-orochi''. The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo had arrived. With his own ingenuity, Susanoo slays the serpent and gains Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine is erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm] </ref>
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}} {{Japan-struct-stub}} {{reli-struct-stub}}
[[ja:八重垣神社]]

{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End

{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End

The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.

The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]

The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}} | image = Yaegaki jinja.jpg | caption = Yaegaki Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|25|44.53|N|133|4|25.26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Matsue]], [[Shimane Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End

{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End
==External links== *[http://www.shinbutsu.jp/45.html Official website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111228094024/http://www.shinbutsu.jp/45.html |date=2011-12-28 }}
<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}

{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:معبد يايغاكي]][[co:commons:Category:Yaegaki-jinja]][[en:Yaegaki Shrine]][[fr:Yaegaki-jinja]][[ja:八重垣神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:معبد يايغاكي]][[co:commons:Category:Yaegaki-jinja]][[en:Yaegaki Shrine]][[fr:Yaegaki-jinja]][[ja:八重垣神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:معبد يايغاكي]][[sn:commons:Category:Yaegaki-jinja]][[en:Yaegaki Shrine]][[fr:Yaegaki-jinja]][[ja:八重垣神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3571285]] Wikidata name: Yaegaki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}} | image = File:Yaegaki jinja.jpg | caption = Yaegaki Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.429028|N|133.073694|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Matsue|WD=Q207321}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in [[:en:Matsue|Matsue]], [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the [[:en:Yamata no Orochi|Yamata-no-orochi]] (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End
==External links== *[http://www.shinbutsu.jp/45.html Official website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111228094024/http://www.shinbutsu.jp/45.html |date=2011-12-28 }}

In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata-no-orochi}} (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:معبد يايغاكي]][[co:commons:Category:Yaegaki-jinja]][[en:Yaegaki Shrine]][[fr:Yaegaki-jinja]][[ja:八重垣神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:معبد يايغاكي]][[co:commons:Category:Yaegaki-jinja]][[en:Yaegaki Shrine]][[fr:Yaegaki-jinja]][[ja:八重垣神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:معبد يايغاكي]][[sn:commons:Category:Yaegaki-jinja]][[en:Yaegaki Shrine]][[fr:Yaegaki-jinja]][[ja:八重垣神社]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Shimane Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}} | image = File:Yaegaki jinja.jpg | caption = Yaegaki Shrine. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.429028|N|133.073694|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style ={{ill|Taisha-zukuri|WD=Q387874}} | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Matsue|WD=Q207321}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Yaegaki Shrine'''|八重垣神社|Yaegaki Jinja}}, formerly known as {{nihongo|'''Sakusa Shrine'''|佐久佐神社|Sakusa Jinja}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Matsue|en|Matsue|WD=Q207321|lt=Matsue}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. [[File:Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine.JPG|thumb|200px|Kagaminoike of Yaegaki Shrine]]
== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata-no-orochi}} (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End
==External links== *[http://www.shinbutsu.jp/45.html Official website] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111228094024/http://www.shinbutsu.jp/45.html |date=2011-12-28 }}
<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> ]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}

In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata-no-orochi}} (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.

== History == The gods ''[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]'' and princess ''[[Kushinadahime|Kushinada-hime]]'' are enshrined here. This shrine is dedicated to marriage and matchmaking. The people who come to this shrine often pray for a marriage partner, good marital relations, pregnancy and healthy child-bearing. In keeping with this theme, several large wooden phalluses can be found on the shrine's grounds.
According to legend, Susanoo came across a couple who had lost seven of their eight daughters to an eight-headed serpent, the {{ill|Yamata no Orochi|en|Yamata no Orochi|WD=Q1054437|lt=Yamata-no-orochi|1=Yamata no Orochi}} (lit., "Eight-Pronged-Serpent"). The serpent was due to return for the last daughter, Princess Kushinada, when Susanoo arrived. Through his ingenuity, Susanoo slew the serpent and won Kushinada's hand in marriage.
The Yaegaki Shrine was erected in the spot where Susanoo built a house for himself and Kushinada to live.
At the shrine, visitors can see the Mirror Pond. One can place a paper on the water's surface and a divining coin on the paper; the amount of time the coin stays afloat on the paper is supposed to indicate the fate of one's marriage.
In the shrine's treasure house is an ancient depiction of the enshrined Princess Inata, painted on Japanese Cypress. It is believed to be the oldest shrine wall mural in Japan. [http://www.city.matsue.shimane.jp/kankou/jp/e/yaegaki.htm]
[http://miya-travelog.tumblr.com/post/373946266/yaegaki-shrine-next-shrine-is]- Journey To The End
